The plague of leprosy was considered an uncleanness rather than a disease. It is said that Christ cleansed lepers, not cured them. Although leprosy was common among the Hebrews during and after their time in Egypt, we have no reason to believe they knew about it before then. Their difficult circumstances and work in that land likely made them susceptible to disease. However, it was often inflicted directly by God's hand. Miriam’s leprosy, Gehazi’s, and King Uzziah’s were punishments for specific sins; there was nothing surprising about the care taken to distinguish it from a common illness. The judgment of this condition was referred to the priests. It was a representation of the moral pollutions of people's minds by sin, which is the leprosy of the soul, defiling to the conscience, and from which Christ alone can cleanse. The priest could only determine that someone had leprosy (which is understanding sin according to the law), but Christ can cure the sinner; he can take away sin. It is a work of great importance, but also of great difficulty, to judge our spiritual state. We all have reason to suspect ourselves, being aware of sores and spots; but whether we are clean or unclean is the question. Just as there were certain marks by which it was known to be leprosy, so there are signs of those who are in the bitterness of gall. The priest must take time to make his judgment. This teaches everyone, both ministers and people, not to be hasty in judgments, nor to judge anything before its proper time. If some people’s sins precede them to judgment, the sins of others follow after, and so do people's good works. If a person suspected of having leprosy was found to be clean, they still must wash their clothes because there had been reason for suspicion. We need to be washed in the blood of Christ from our spots, even if they are not leprosy spots; for who can say, I am pure from sin?

The priest is instructed about what judgment to make if there appears to be leprosy in old sores; and this is the danger faced by those who have escaped the pollutions of the world but become entangled again. Or, in a burn caused by an accident, verse #. The burning of strife and contention often causes that corruption to rise and break out, proving that people are unclean. Human life lies exposed to many grievances; and everything is entered by sin! If our health is good, and our bodies lively and easy, we should glorify God with them. Particular attention was given to leprosy if it appeared on the head. If the leprosy of sin has seized the head; if judgment is corrupted, and wicked principles that support wicked practices are embraced, it is utter uncleanness, from which few are cleansed. Soundness in faith keeps leprosy from the head.

When the priest declared a person unclean with leprosy, it stopped their business in the world, cut them off from friends and family, and ruined all comfort they could have. They must humble themselves under God’s mighty hand, not insisting on their cleanness when the priest had pronounced them unclean, but accepting the punishment. We must take to ourselves the shame that belongs to us, and with broken hearts call ourselves “Unclean, unclean;” heart unclean, life unclean; unclean by original corruption, unclean by actual transgression; unclean, therefore deserving to be forever shut out from communion with God, and all hope of happiness in him; unclean, therefore undone, if infinite mercy does not intervene. The leper must warn others to take care when approaching them. Then they must be shut out of the camp, and later, when entering Canaan, be shut out of the city, town, or village where they lived, and live with only other lepers like themselves. This represented the purity that should be in the gospel church.

A garment suspected to be tainted with leprosy was not to be burned immediately. If, upon examination, it was found to have a leprous spot, it must be burned, or at least that part of it. If it proved to be free from infection, it must be washed and then could be used. This also illustrates the great evil there is in sin. It not only defiles someone’s conscience but leaves a stain on everything they have and do. And those who use their clothes to express pride and lust may see them tainted with leprosy. But robes of righteousness never wear out, nor are moth-eaten.
